full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Historians have said two things happened in Western Christianity<|5.00|> <|5.00|> as a reaction to the advent of the theory of evolution.<|11.00|> <|11.00|> In Protestantism, there was the fundamentalist movement,<|17.00|> <|17.00|> and in Roman Catholicism, there was the declaration of papal infallibility.<|25.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Those are gross oversimplifications,<|4.00|> <|4.00|> but they demonstrate all of these ideas were swirling in the water<|9.00|> <|9.00|> around the same time.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> One of the fallouts of American Christian fundamentalism<|16.00|> <|16.00|> is the idea that a person or a group of people<|20.00|> <|20.00|> can interpret a text objectively.<|25.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> that there are people, usually white, straight, and male,<|6.88|> <|7.44|> who have the ability to read a text impartially.<|11.94|> <|14.38|> I didn't know y'all would laugh at that.<|16.20|> <|17.60|> I guess we all know that's not the case.<|20.14|> <|21.10|> So they have the ability to read a text impartially<|24.38|> <|24.38|> while the rest of us...<|27.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> read as non-white, non-straight, non-male,<|4.14|> <|5.34|> are subjective interpreters of the text.<|8.80|> <|10.36|> I didn't know y'all were going to be so vocal tonight.<|12.22|> <|12.32|> This is great.<|12.84|> <|13.32|> I've been waiting for this all summer.<|15.00|> <|16.00|> Where have y'all been?<|17.04|> <|17.12|> This is awesome.<|17.90|> <|19.16|> Because I'm like, I've been preaching pretty good sermons,<|21.26|> <|21.34|> and y'all are quiet.<|22.10|> again.<|6.00|> <|6.00|> So while the rest of us are at as non-white, non-straight, non-male,<|9.00|> <|9.00|> are subjective interpreters of the text,<|11.00|> <|11.00|> if I were a certain daytime television personality,<|14.00|> <|14.00|> I'd say, "That is a lie."<|16.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> because it is.<|2.00|> <|2.00|> It is a lie because as we know from various literary theories,<|6.00|> <|6.00|> a person or group of people is never reading or interpreting texts<|12.00|> <|12.00|> without the weight of their own prejudices, memories,<|17.00|> <|17.00|> racial, ethnic, and gender identities, sexual orientation,<|22.00|> <|22.00|> class, educational background, taste in movies, and nationality.<|29.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> To say the Bible says it, I believe it, that settles it,<|5.14|> <|5.14|> is to oversimplify Christianity's most sacred texts.<|11.00|> <|12.36|> A more fitting maxim is we take the Bible too seriously<|19.06|> <|19.06|> to take it literally.<|21.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We take the Bible too seriously to take it literally because we are painfully aware of<|7.90|> <|7.90|> the numerous ways its books, chapters, and verses are used and have been used to make<|14.52|> <|14.52|> the most vulnerable more vulnerable and to make the most powerful more powerful.<|21.14|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For instance, Frederick Douglass, and I just finished reading David Blight's definitive<|6.36|> <|6.36|> biography of Frederick Douglass, which is quite informative and moving.<|13.06|> <|14.46|> Frederick Douglass and other formerly enslaved black Americans spoke ad nauseum about<|20.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> about white Christian preachers coming to their labor camps<|4.00|> <|4.00|> and quoting Ephesians and Colossians<|7.00|> <|7.00|> to justify remaining as the property of white enslavers.<|13.00|> <|14.00|> If you are an enslaver and human trafficker,<|18.00|> <|18.00|> your interpretive lens is shaped accordingly.<|23.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You can't help but read the Bible as an enslaver.<|4.74|> <|4.74|> If you are an LGBTQ person who has been derided and suppressed based on your gender identity or sexual orientation, you can't help but read the Bible through that lens.<|19.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The same principle extends to women, the impoverished, the wealthy, and every other category one can name.<|11.06|> <|12.16|> We all bring our own specific contexts and experiences to sacred texts.<|19.36|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This is important to note when hearing,<|4.32|> <|5.02|> therefore a man leaves his father and his mother<|7.58|> <|7.58|> and clings to his wife and they become one flesh.<|11.82|> <|13.56|> And whoever divorces his wife and marries another<|16.96|> <|16.96|> commits adultery against her.<|18.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is important to note, because as I've heard said before and will say again,<|7.00|> <|7.00|> a text without a context is a pretext for a subtext.<|13.00|> <|13.00|> Not only is the context of a given text important,<|20.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But my understanding the context of my own life is important as I encounter texts held sacred by Christian faith.<|12.82|> <|14.42|> In other words, it helps to be self-aware when reading the Bible.<|20.58|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is worth our time for just a moment<|3.52|> <|3.52|> to meditate on the dim and uncreative ways<|7.76|> <|7.76|> texts like Genesis and Mark<|10.42|> <|10.42|> are used to marginalize and stigmatize<|14.68|> <|14.68|> divorced and LGBTQ people.<|17.78|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.54|> From there, it is worthwhile imagining<|7.14|> <|7.14|> that if sacred texts can be used to denigrate, deride,<|11.88|> <|11.88|> and dehumanize vulnerable people,<|14.88|> <|15.98|> they can also be used for the liberation and freedom<|21.36|> <|21.36|> of divorced and LGBTQ people.<|24.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.16|> I mentioned Frederick Douglass and other black Americans<|4.88|> <|4.88|> just a moment ago.<|6.12|> <|7.82|> Against all odds, black Americans on labor camps,<|12.82|> <|13.56|> sometimes called plantations,<|15.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> all over this and other lands,<|3.32|> <|4.28|> used not only the sacred texts of scripture,<|7.36|> <|8.24|> but the sacred texts of their own lives<|11.14|> <|11.14|> to write and embody a new story of freedom<|14.92|> <|14.92|> for themselves and for generations to come.<|19.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.00|> they could ask in full-throated ways,<|5.20|> <|5.20|> "If it is not good news for the crucified peoples of the world,<|10.40|> <|10.40|> then who is it good news for?"<|13.20|> <|14.36|> That question causes us to pause,<|18.00|> <|19.20|> and before breaking open books to use as bludgeons,<|23.60|> <|23.60|> ask ourselves,<|25.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> as I absorb, chew on, and digest this text,<|6.66|> <|7.80|> am I bringing to it a generous, life-giving interpretation<|14.22|> <|14.22|> that will benefit as many people as possible?<|17.60|> <|19.60|> If not, God, will you help me do so?<|24.22|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> you<|1.18|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> distilled to its most basic form.<|4.90|> <|6.40|> Perhaps that is all a baptized person,<|9.78|> <|9.78|> a Christian person is ever asking God.<|13.24|> <|14.92|> God, make me generous.<|16.96|> <|18.02|> God, make me useful.<|20.36|> <|20.36|> God, make me a staging area for the new creation<|24.52|> <|24.52|> experienced in Christ's death and resurrection.<|28.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And when we take that posture, we will have changed the whole world. Amen.<|8.36|><|endoftext|>
